title: Signals and Systems
summary: This page discusses signal energy and power as measures of signal size, clarifying that these are normalized concepts based on a 1-ohm resistor load rather than actual physical energy. It explains the utility of these measures in approximation error analysis and signal-to-noise ratio evaluation in communication systems. The page also addresses the dimensional units of signal energy and power depending on whether the signal is a voltage or current.

figures.caption: Examples of signals: (a) a signal with finite energy and (b) a signal with finite power.
figures.description: The figure has two time-domain plots with horizontal axis labeled t and signal amplitude labeled x(t). In (a), the waveform is a single pulse-like signal that rises from zero, peaks, and decays back toward zero, illustrating a finite-energy signal concentrated over time. In (b), the waveform oscillates above and below the horizontal axis over the entire time range, illustrating a finite-power signal that persists rather than dying out. Students should notice the contrast between a transient signal and a continuing oscillatory signal.
